From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

For the novel in the Darren Shan series, see [Cirque du Freak](/wiki/Cirque_du_Freak "Cirque du Freak"). For the novel this film is based from, see [The Vampire's Assistant](/wiki/The_Vampire%27s_Assistant "The Vampire's Assistant").

2009 American film

***Cirque du Freak: The Vampire's Assistant*** is a 2009 American [fantasy film](/wiki/Fantasy_film "Fantasy film") directed by [Paul Weitz](/wiki/Paul_Weitz_(filmmaker) "Paul Weitz (filmmaker)") and co-screenplay adapted with Brian Helgeland, loosely based on the *[Vampire Blood](/wiki/Vampire_Blood "Vampire Blood")* trilogy of the book series *[The Saga of Darren Shan](/wiki/The_Saga_of_Darren_Shan "The Saga of Darren Shan")* by author [Darren Shan](/wiki/Darren_Shan "Darren Shan"). The first three books in the 12-part series—*[Cirque du Freak](/wiki/Cirque_du_Freak "Cirque du Freak")*, *The Vampire’s Assistant*, and *[Tunnels of Blood](/wiki/Tunnels_of_Blood "Tunnels of Blood")*—inspired the film. The film was released by [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") on October 23, 2009, received poor reviews and was a commercial failure by grossing $39.2 million against a $40 million budget.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cirque_du_Freak:_The_Vampire%27s_Assistant&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In an unnamed American town, teenagers Darren Shan and Steve Leonard, Darren's more rebellious best friend and classmate, sneak out at night to attend the Cirque du Freak [freak show](/wiki/Freak_show "Freak show") at a local theater. During the performance, Steve recognizes one of the troupe's members, Larten Crepsley, as Ver Hosten, a vampire who is over a hundred years old, prompting him to try to find Crepsley after the show. Meanwhile, Darren, fascinated by Crepsley's spider, Madam Octa, goes backstage and impulsively steals her but is forced to hide when Crepsley returns to his [green room](/wiki/Green_room "Green room"). Steve arrives, begging to be made into a vampire, but Crepsley refuses after tasting Steve's blood, which he says "tastes of evil." When Crepsley realizes that Madam Octa was stolen, Darren flees from the theater with Crepsley in pursuit. He escapes when the mysterious Mr. Tiny arrives in his limousine, accompanied by Murlough. The latter is unimpressed with Darren, dismissing him as a "bag of blood," but Tiny promises to stay in touch after dropping Darren off at his home.

The following day, Darren secretly brings Madam Octa to school, where she escapes. A struggle in a crowded hallway follows and Steve tries to kill the spider, but she bites him on the cheek, leaving him to succumb to her deadly venom in the hospital. Darren returns to the theater and begs Crepsley for help. Initially annoyed and contemptuous, Crepsley agrees on the condition that Darren becomes a half-vampire—and Crepsley's personal assistant. Darren agrees, only to flee while Crepsley administers the antidote to Steve. However, after nearly attacking his own sister out of vampiric bloodlust, Darren decides to leave with Crepsley, who uses magic to stage Darren's death of a fall from a roof.

After being buried in a cemetery, Crepsley digs up the grave to free Darren, but Murlough ambushes them. Crepsley fights him off, and the two go to the Cirque du Freak campgrounds, where Darren meets Evra Von, the snake boy, and Rebecca, the monkey girl. Meanwhile, Steve, contemplating suicide after losing his best friend, is stopped by Tiny, who offers him a chance to become a Vampaneze, a race of vampires who—unlike Crepsley and others—murder their victims to feed on their blood. Steve agrees, proceeding to kill high school history teacher Mr. Kersey with Murlough's help. Trying to instigate a confrontation between Steve and Darren, Tiny kidnaps Rebecca and Darren's family, leaving a flyer for the Cirque du Freak at Darren's former home.

At the theater, Crepsley and Murlough fight while Darren fights with Steve, but Darren's refusal to feed weakens him. Rebecca frees herself using her monkey tail and offers Darren a taste of her blood. After some hesitation, he accepts. Crepsley stabs Murlough, and with his dying words, Murlough declares that the truce between the two vampire clans has been broken. Tiny (who had been watching from the balcony via opera glasses) eventually separates Steve and Darren. Darren asks Steve to stay with him, but Steve refuses, saying, "I have my destiny, and you have yours." Steve then leaves with Tiny, who plans to groom him as a Vampaneze leader.

Crepsley returns Darren's family to their home, hypnotizing them so they'll forget what they've been through. Rebecca and Darren share a heartfelt kiss before returning to the campgrounds, where Crepsley gives Darren a coffin to sleep in, and Darren accepts his new life as a member of the Cirque du Freak.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cirque_du_Freak:_The_Vampire%27s_Assistant&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

*Cirque du Freak: The Vampire's Assistant* was filmed between February 19 and June 1, 2008, in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), [Folsom](/wiki/Folsom,_Louisiana "Folsom, Louisiana"), and [Baton Rouge](/wiki/Baton_Rouge "Baton Rouge"), [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"). Some of the characters required extensive [prosthetics](/wiki/Prosthetic "Prosthetic") and makeup, which was led by Steve Koch, Brian Sipe, and Mark Garbarino. Prosthetics did not quite add the height needed on certain shots for the character of Mr. Tall, played by Academy Award-nominated [Ken Watanabe](/wiki/Ken_Watanabe "Ken Watanabe").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Although Watanabe is six feet tall, a [body double](/wiki/Body_double "Body double") was cast for some scenes to accentuate the abnormal height of the character Mr. Tall. Trevon Flores, a local basketball player who stands 6'10" tall and weighs 210 pounds, was used cast as the body double. Watanabe also used dialogue coaches [Kathleen S. Dunn](/wiki/Kathleen_S._Dunn "Kathleen S. Dunn") and Francie Brown in pre-production and production to further enhance his performance as the circus barker. [Computer-generated imagery](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") was also used to portray other fantasy elements.

[John Marshall High School](/wiki/John_Marshall_High_School_(Los_Angeles) "John Marshall High School (Los Angeles)") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") was used to film some parts of the movie. [Sophie B. Wright Charter School](/wiki/Sophie_B._Wright_Charter_School "Sophie B. Wright Charter School") in New Orleans was also used to shoot scenes from the film. A portion was filmed on a set constructed within New Orleans City Park, approximately 1,000 feet off the side of the road, along Harrison Avenue.

[Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") began on February 8, 2008, in New Orleans and concluded on June 3, 2008.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The film was distributed by [Universal Studios](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"). In one of the manga additions of the saga, the director says that the character of Gavner Purl was a hint of the sequel he wanted to make.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cirque_du_Freak:_The_Vampire%27s_Assistant&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Music")]

The [score](/wiki/Film_score "Film score") to *The Vampire's Assistant* was composed by [Stephen Trask](/wiki/Stephen_Trask "Stephen Trask"), marking his third feature-film with director Paul Weitz. The score was recorded with an 86-piece ensemble of the [Hollywood Studio Symphony](/wiki/Hollywood_Studio_Symphony "Hollywood Studio Symphony") at the Newman Scoring Stage at [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox").[[6]](#cite_note-scoring-6) The movie also features the songs "[Something Is Not Right with Me](/wiki/Something_Is_Not_Right_with_Me "Something Is Not Right with Me")" by [Cold War Kids](/wiki/Cold_War_Kids "Cold War Kids"), "[Chelsea Dagger](/wiki/Chelsea_Dagger "Chelsea Dagger")" by [The Fratellis](/wiki/The_Fratellis "The Fratellis"), and "[Red Right Hand](/wiki/Red_Right_Hand "Red Right Hand")" by [Nick Cave](/wiki/Nick_Cave "Nick Cave"). The trailer features the songs *Asleep From Day* by [The Chemical Brothers](/wiki/The_Chemical_Brothers "The Chemical Brothers"), *Bliss* by [Syntax](/wiki/Syntax_(band) "Syntax (band)"), and *Superhero* by [Immediate Music](/wiki/Immediate_Music "Immediate Music").

For the Japanese dub, the film received its own theme song, "Can We Go Back", sung by [Koda Kumi](/wiki/Koda_Kumi "Koda Kumi").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cirque_du_Freak:_The_Vampire%27s_Assistant&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Release")]

The film was originally set for release on January 15, 2010, but was moved to an earlier release date of October 23, 2009.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cirque_du_Freak:_The_Vampire%27s_Assistant&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

Review aggregation website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") assigns the film a rating of 37% based on 138 critics with an average rating of 4.87/10. The site's critical consensus reads "This overstuffed, scattershot vampire flick suffers from poor characterization and an unwieldy mix of scares and chuckles."[[14]](#cite_note-14) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the film has a score of 43 out of 100, based on 25 reviews, indicating "mixed or average" reviews.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "B" on an A+ to F scale.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cirque_du_Freak:_The_Vampire%27s_Assistant&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Box office")]

The film opened in 2,754 theaters in the United States and made over $14 million, reaching #7 in the charts. In other countries, it made more than $25 million, giving it a worldwide box office total of more than $39 million. On DVD, sales in the United States made more than $5.5 million.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The film's chart placings worldwide include reaching #1 in Ukraine, #2 in Hungary, #2 in Russia, #2 in the United Arab Emirates, #3 in Mexico, #3 in Portugal, #4 in Egypt, #4 in Venezuela, #5 in Belgium, #5 in Peru, #5 in Singapore, #5 in the United Kingdom, #6 in Lebanon, #6 in the Philippines, #7 in Bulgaria, #7 in Japan, #8 in Chile, #8 in Colombia, #8 in the Netherlands, #9 in Austria, #9 in Romania, #11 in Germany, #11 in Malaysia, #11 in New Zealand.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cirque_du_Freak:_The_Vampire%27s_Assistant&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Home media")]

The film debuted on DVD and Blu-ray Disc in Canada, the United Kingdom and United States at the end of February 2010.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In Canada, by the end of its first week on sale and available to rent, it was #1 on the Rogers DVD bestselling chart, #2 on the Blockbuster Canada bestselling chart, and #6 on the rental charts of both. In the United States it was #2 on the Rentrak bestseller chart, and #6 on the Blockbuster, Home Media and IMDb rental charts. In the United Kingdom it reached #5 on the MyMovies bestsellers chart, and #6 on the Yahoo chart.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Canceled sequels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cirque_du_Freak:_The_Vampire%27s_Assistant&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Canceled sequels")]

In a [Reddit AMA](/wiki/Reddit_AMA "Reddit AMA") in March 2015, Darren Shan stated that three sequels to *Cirque du Freak: The Vampire's Assistant* had been abandoned by Universal prior to the film's failure, as well as expressing interest in a potential future [reboot](/wiki/Reboot_(fiction) "Reboot (fiction)") of the series.[[22]](#cite_note-22)
